wikidata_org_candidates.label: Radio Corporation of America
wikidata_org_candidates.description: former American electronics company (1919-1969), subsidiary of General Electric
wikidata_org_candidates.reasoning: The name 'Radio Corporation of America' on the card is an exact match for the official name of the Wikidata entity. The card describes the employer's field as 'Electronic engineering' and the Wikidata entity is described as a former American electronics company based in New York City, which matches the geographic location of the individual on the card (Brooklyn, New York).

wikidata_org_candidates.label: RCA connector
wikidata_org_candidates.description: electrical connector with one signal channel mainly used for analog stereo audio (in pairs), analog component or composite video and rarely for digital audio
wikidata_org_candidates.reasoning: The Wikidata entity Q399653 represents the RCA connector, which is a physical audio/video product. The employer listed on the card is the Radio Corporation of America, which is the company that designed the connector, but the entities themselves (a product vs. an organization) are distinct types.

wikidata_org_candidates.label: RCA Corporation

The industry (electronics) and headquarters (New York) also match the card's context.

results.possibleIdentity: Joseph Cataland was an electronic engineer associated with the Radio Corporation of America (RCA) in Brooklyn, New York, during the late 1960s. Records from the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) identify him as a student electronic engineer at RCA. His involvement with E.A.T., a non-profit established to foster collaboration between the arts and technology, suggests he was part of the directory of engineers who offered technical assistance to artists. In later years, a person by the same name (sometimes cited as Joseph Catalano) was active as an artist and illustrator, notably creating sports memorabilia such as a 1991 Troy Aikman mini-plate for Sports Impressions. Biographical records indicate individuals named Joseph Cataland of an appropriate age for a student in the late 1960s have lived in locations including Santa Ana, California, and Bradenton, Florida.
